title: Today's AI Brief: The Vatican’s Man Inside Anthropic
summary: A look at the Vatican's involvement in AI and recent announcements from Google I/O 2026.
imageAlt: Image from source article for The Vatican’s Man Inside Anthropic

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and welcome to Today's AI Brief. I've been digging into some interesting developments in the world of artificial intelligence. The Vatican's involvement in AI has raised some eyebrows, and Google's I/O 2026 conference was packed with announcements.

First up, Chris Olah, an atheist and former Google researcher, has been making waves as a speaker at a ceremony following Pope Leo's historic AI encyclical. The Pope called for 'disarming' AI technology, which seems like a lofty goal. What's interesting here is that Olah, now at Anthropic, is part of the inner circle. His presence suggests that the Vatican is taking a keen interest in the ethics of AI development.

Next, Google's I/O 2026 conference was a flurry of activity, with 100 announcements, launches, and demos. I'm not going to go through all 100, but it's clear that Google is pushing hard on AI integration across its platforms. From model updates to new tools, there's a lot to unpack.

Also on the board is the ongoing conversation around AI safety, trust, and distribution. As AI becomes more pervasive, these issues are coming to the fore. My read is that we're seeing a platform shift in AI, with more emphasis on practical applications and less on just pushing the tech forward.

My quick read: the artificial intelligence story keeps getting bigger, but also stranger in very human ways. One moment it is strategy and infrastructure; the next, the Vatican is in the room. That is a pretty good sign this technology has left the lab and wandered into the town square.

sources.title: AI Rings on Fingers Can Interpret Sign Language
